text: The surgeon begins the hernia repair in a retrorectus location, taking down attachments to the semilunar line to access the preperitoneal space. A 30-degree camera is used for visualization. Medial dissection is performed down to Cooper's ligament and the pubic bone.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the spermatic cord, aiming to avoid unnecessary bleeding that could obscure the tissue plane. The fourth robotic arm is utilized to assist in the dissection process.

The surgeon carefully dissects down onto the peritoneum, revealing an additional tissue layer. The gonadal vessels and cord structures are visibly swept off the sliding retroperitoneal component of the large indirect hernia. The sigmoid colon is observed within the hernia sac.

The surgeon attempts to reduce the entire hernia sac, carefully navigating through fibrotic tissue layers. The area appears complex with multiple layers, and there is visible caution to avoid damaging the bowel protruding through the defect.

The surgeon continues to reduce the hernia sac, working through the fibrotic capsule. As the dissection progresses, a large portion of the hernia sac visibly descends, indicating successful reduction.

The surgeon sweeps the cord structures posteriorly and laterally, ensuring they are safely positioned. The dissection continues to locate the vas deferens to safely take down the remaining hernia sac. A scarred segment is visible in the medial aspect of the canal, and the surgeon identifies the edge of the hernia sac through the fibrotic tissue.
The surgeon checks the surgical field before proceeding with the parietalization of the cord structures, completing the dissection at the left end of the heel.

The camera is positioned to the left of the midline, just above the umbilicus, providing a view into the canal where the cord and vas are visible without any cord lipoma. The surgeon completes the pocket and prepares to place a 16x12 ProGrip mesh inside.
